text: In this video, we present a robotic repair of colovascular fistula due to complicated Crohn's disease. The patient was a forty-seven-year-old woman who presented with abdominal pain, vomiting and fever. She reported pneumaturia during the last two weeks. Her past medical history was significant for Crohn's disease, for which the patient was on oral mesalazine. Abdominal CT and MR enterography revealed colovascular fistula with severe narrowing of the corresponding colonic lumen.

In this video, we present a robotic repair of colovascular fistula due to complicated Crohn's disease. The patient was a forty-seven-year-old woman who presented with abdominal pain, vomiting and fever. She reported pneumaturia during the last two weeks. Her past medical history was significant for Crohn's disease, for which the patient was on oral mesalazine. Abdominal CT and MR enterography revealed colovascular fistula with severe narrowing of the corresponding colonic lumen.

The surgeon identifies the interovascular fistula between the descending colon and the bladder dome, mobilizes it circumferentially, and divides it. The descending colon is retracted cranially, and the medial peritoneal fold is incised. Dissection proceeds laterally along the toes fascia, with the left ureter identified and protected. Sigmoid arteries are progressively divided, and the proximal rectum is prepared. The lateral peritoneal fold is incised, and the section is extended cranially to partially immobilize the splenic flexure.
The surgeon identifies the interovascular fistula located between the descending colon and the dome of the bladder. The fistula is then mobilized circumferentially and divided.

The surgeon retracts the descending colon cranially and incises the medial peritoneal fold. Dissection proceeds laterally along the Toldt's fascia, with the left ureter identified and protected. Sigmoid arteries are progressively divided, and the proximal rectum is prepared. The lateral peritoneal fold is incised, and the section is extended cranially to partially mobilize the splenic flexure.

The surgeon divides the proximal rectum using a linear stapler. The bladder is mobilized, and the defect is repaired in two layers using absorbable sutures. Methylene blue is instilled into the bladder to check the effectiveness of the closure.

The surgeon divides the proximal rectum using a linear stapler. The bladder is mobilized, and the defect is repaired in two layers using absorbable sutures. Methylene blue is instilled into the bladder to check the effectiveness of the closure.
The uterus is suspended with external traction. The surgical specimen is retrieved through a funnel-shaped incision, and a transcenial anastomosis is performed using a circular stapler.
The uterus is suspended with external traction. The surgical specimen is retrieved through a funnel-shaped incision. A transanal anastomosis is performed using a circular stapler.
